title: The Rules of the Road
subtitle: Compliance as a spec

hook.metaphor: Traffic laws aren't there to slow you down for fun — they encode hard-won lessons about what keeps everyone safe. Compliance regimes are the same: each clause is a requirement you can read as a product spec, not legalese to fear.
hook.scenario: A customer emails: 'Delete everything you hold about me.' That single sentence is a legal right (GDPR's erasure, India's DPDP). The question for you isn't 'is this allowed?' — it's 'can our product actually find, and provably delete, every copy?'

learn: You don't need to be a lawyer. You need to read regulations as **capabilities your product must support**. The major regimes — **GDPR** (EU), **HIPAA** (US health), **CCPA/CPRA** (California), **DPDP** (India) — differ in detail but rhyme in what they demand.

The recurring demands, as features: **consent** (capture and honor a lawful basis), **DSARs / access requests** (show a person their data on demand), **right to erasure** (delete it, everywhere, provably), **data residency** (keep data in-region), **retention limits** (don't keep it forever), and **breach notification** (tell regulators/users within a clock).

The builder's move is translation: every clause → a concrete capability with a data model and an API. 'Right to be forgotten' isn't a paragraph; it's a deletion workflow that traverses lineage (remember Stop 1's map).
buildBridge: So your product must **translate regulatory clauses into enforceable capabilities** — consent records, DSAR fulfilment, verifiable deletion, residency rules — rather than treating compliance as a PDF someone signs.

seeAlso.label: 🧭 See exactly which rules apply to your product — open the Navigator
🌍 Browse the global DRC Atlas — every regulation, framework & standard, and which are region-specific

interactions.prompt: Match each regulatory requirement to the product capability it demands.

pairs.right: Traverse lineage and provably delete every copy
Assemble and export everything you hold on a person
Pin storage/processing to a specific region
Detect, then alert within a fixed time clock

interactions.prompt: Your product launches in the EU, US, and India. How do you handle their different rules?

options.label: Build a separate app per region
options.feedback: Triple the maintenance, triple the drift. Regimes rhyme — model the common capabilities once.

options.label: Model capabilities once, make the *policy* configurable per region
options.feedback: Yes. One consent engine, one deletion workflow — driven by region-specific policy. This is 'compliance as configuration,' not forked code.

options.label: Comply with the strictest one everywhere and ignore the rest
options.feedback: Tempting, but residency and lawful-basis rules can actively conflict — 'strictest' isn't always a superset.

teachBack: In one line: *Read every regulation as a product spec — consent, access, erasure, residency, notification — and model the capability once, configured per region.*

checkpoint.question: What's the builder's right way to treat a compliance regime?
options: As legalese only the legal team handles
As a set of capabilities your product must support, expressed as features
As a one-time certification you pass and forget
checkpoint.explanation: Clauses become consent records, DSAR flows, deletion workflows — buildable things.

checkpoint.question: Why model capabilities once and configure policy per region?
options: Because regimes rhyme — shared capabilities, region-specific policy avoids forked, drifting code
checkpoint.explanation: One deletion/consent engine driven by per-region policy = 'compliance as configuration.'
